paragraphs.body: Many Different Kinds of Cold Cases

In the podcast, Cold Case Files, narrated by Brooke Gittings, she released an episode titled, “The Black Dahlia.” In this episode Gittings reminds us about the unsolved cold case of a young and upcoming actor in Hollywood, Elizabeth Short. She supports her point by developing several important ideas. First, by acknowledging the explicit details that surrounded Elizabeth Short's murder, such as the mutilation of her body and including her life leading up to the murder. Gittings also gave her listeners insight to the kind of person Elizabeth Short was before she was brutally killed and staged. Next, she advocates for the theories surrounding the upcoming actress’s death and the list of potential suspects. Finally, she encourages the listeners of the podcast to make their own conclusions. She allows for this to be done from the details she went over in the episode.

annotations.context_left: developing several important ideas. First, by acknowledging the explicit details that surrounded

paragraphs.body: Elizabeth Short was a beautiful up and coming girl in California, so the question is why is it that she was murdered? As was mentioned earlier, the questions are racing through my mind. After listening to the podcast, I was able to come up with my own conclusions about who killed her. I believe that George Hodel was Elizabeth’s killer. The facts presented in the podcast led me to that inquiry. Gittings presented in the episode that homicide detective Steve Hodel suspected his father George Hodel to be the killer. According to Gittings,”Steve believes his father murdered Elizabeth Short.” Gittings point is that George Hodel is a good candidate for being the one to have murdered Elizabeth. Afterwards, the quote was followed up with details as to why he is a good candidate. She goes on to say how there were photos found of a girl who looked just like Elizabeth in his fathers things and his father was also a doctor. Elizabeth had a rough life growing up and leading to her death. She suffered from asthma, no father in the picture, living through the great depression, trouble with the police, bounced from relationship to relationship, and was known to lie. However, she still was determined to make something of herself and to be known. Just not in the way she thought.

paragraphs.body: After I first learned about cold cases I became obsessed. I have always been a sucker for true crime including, the television shows, documentaries, and the videos that popped up while scrolling through TikTok. When I first learned about “The Black Dahlia” I became intensely interested and involved. I didn’t know why I was enthralled with this cold case, but I was and ended up reading and watching anything I could about her murder. Now, this was years ago when I first learned about Elizabeth Short. However, I rediscovered this murder case once again. I was at first wondering as to why I was so interested and drawn in until I made my connection. This may be an odd connection one might make, but I now understand.

annotations.text: After I first learned about cold cases I became obsessed.

paragraphs.body: I never finish anything if I can help it. Books, a new craft, or a television series. There will always be a chapter left in any book I read for my own enjoyment, one part left to finish in a craft, and one more episode to watch. I didn’t know why I did this until I began going to therapy for my seasonal depression. My therapist and I would have conversation after conversation that seemed to go nowhere. That was until I began opening up and she was now asking different questions. She started to notice my pattern about not finishing things when I was so close to it. We spent session after session trying to crack this code. Then it happened. She asked the one question I didn’t want to address which was about my mother. My mom is my biggest role model now, but as a child that was a different story. When I was younger I was living my life as usual going to school, fighting with my sisters, and begging my parents for a dog until it all quickly changed. It was April and I walked home after I got off the bus like usual excited to tell my mom about my day. When I reached the house I saw her in the driveway sobbing and I became concerned because no one likes to see their mom cry. I reached her and she held me and sobbed into my ear,”Aunt Missy died.” I suddenly didn't know how to react and began fake crying because I didn’t really have a relationship with my aunt and didn’t want to upset my mom. After that it’s all a blur. Memories of her stumbling around the house shouting hurtful words at my sisters and I, finding her passed out in the garage at 6pm because I was wondering if we were going to eat dinner that night, and not being able to fall asleep at night because she was at a bar and not laying with me. I quickly grew up after I watched from my sister's window while my grandmother held me down as I was scream crying,”Mommy! Mommy! Please don’t take my mommy,” as we watched her get dragged away by the police. I didn’t see her until the following Sunday in a weird room of a building that almost resembled a hospital. I found out she was an alcoholic and went to rehab. I don’t remember much other than a few things about that time of my life, but I do remember how fast I matured and lost my childhood. I learned to be my own parent. I rarely acted out when she came back, so she didn’t need to worry about me when my sisters were enough for her to handle. I don’t hate my mom and she is truthfully the best mom ever, but I do wish she intervened and allowed me to be a kid. I associate feeling unfinished in my childhood as my own personal cold case. I think that is why I am so enthralled and obsessed with cold cases because the ones I am intrigued the most don’t have an ending. I am now 18 years old and have completely forgiven my mom after many conversations. I will say that I am not afraid to hide my feelings anymore and that it is perfectly normal and okay to feel them. Even though I know I can’t go back and get the years and experiences I missed doesn’t mean I can’t have a taste of what it would be like. In the past two years or so I started to let my inner child loose. I’ve been eating ice cream cake on my birthdays, going to trampoline parks with my little cousins, even taking my younger cousins to their friends birthday parties to just soak in the atmosphere of childlike wonder surrounding me. I know these experiences won’t make up for what I missed entirely, but it allows me to imagine what it would have been like for me when I was younger and help me to heal. PodcastOne. PodcastOne: The Black Dahlia. https://www.podcastone.com/episode/The-Black-Dahlia.
